source_paper_title: Inexact Bregman Proximal Gradient Method and Its Inertial Variant with Absolute and Partial Relative Stopping Criteria
context:
Page 9, Section 3 (discussion following Algorithm 1; paragraph comparing (AbSC) vs (ReSC))

“In our current convergence analysis, we also require \(\|\Delta_k\| = 0\) for (ReSC) to eliminate the error term \(|\langle\Delta_k, \tilde x_{k+1} - x\rangle|\) in (3.7), enabling the derivation of (3.14) in the subsequent analysis for Theorem 3.2. Although this requirement can be satisfied in many cases (see two examples in the following subsection), it may limit the applicability of (ReSC). Note that Bello-Cruz et al. [18] recently incorporated a full relative-type error rule in the FISTA, which can accommodate a nonzero error \(\Delta_k\). Thus, it would be interesting to explore whether the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\| = 0\) can be removed in our methods, which we will leave for future research.”
title: Removing the zero-dual-error requirement in the partial relative stopping criterion for iBPGM
latex:
Consider the convex composite optimization problem
\[
\min_{x\in Q}\;F(x):=P(x)+f(x),
\]
where \(Q\subseteq E\) is a closed convex set in a finite-dimensional Euclidean space \(E\), \(P:E\to(-\infty,+\infty]\) is proper closed convex, and \(f:E\to(-\infty,+\infty]\) is proper closed convex and differentiable on \(\mathrm{int}\,Q\). Let \(\phi:E\to(-\infty,+\infty]\) be an essentially smooth, strictly convex kernel with \(\mathrm{dom}\,\phi=Q\), and define the Bregman distance
\[
D_\phi(u,v):=\phi(u)-\phi(v)-\langle \nabla\phi(v),u-v\rangle.
\]
Assume \(f\) is \(L\)-smooth relative to \(\phi\) (possibly restricted to a closed convex set \(X\supseteq \mathrm{dom}\,P\cap \mathrm{dom}\,\phi\)) in the sense that for all \(x\in X\cap \mathrm{int}\,Q\) and \(y\in X\cap Q\),
\[
 f(y)\le f(x)+\langle \nabla f(x),y-x\rangle + L D_\phi(y,x).
\]
At iteration \(k\), define the Bregman proximal-gradient subproblem
\[
\min_{x\in Q}\; P(x)+\langle \nabla f(x^k),x-x^k\rangle + \lambda D_\phi(x,x^k),
\]
with parameter \(\lambda>L\). An inexact two-point approximate solution consists of \(x^{k+1}\in X\cap \mathrm{int}\,Q\), \(\tilde x^{k+1}\in \mathrm{dom}\,P\cap Q\), and error pair \((\Delta_k,\delta_k)\) satisfying
\[
\Delta_k\in \partial_{\delta_k}P(\tilde x^{k+1})+\nabla f(x^k)+\lambda\big(\nabla\phi(x^{k+1})-\nabla\phi(x^k)\big).
\]
The paper’s partial relative stopping criterion (ReSC) requires \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) and
\[
\lambda^{-1}\delta_k + D_\phi(\tilde x^{k+1},x^{k+1})\le \sigma\, D_\phi(\tilde x^{k+1},x^k)
\quad\text{for some }\sigma\in\big[0,1-\tfrac{L}{\lambda}\big).
\]
Problem: establish convergence (e.g., \(F(\tilde x^k)\to \inf_Q F\) and an \(O(1/k)\) rate under suitable summable/relative error conditions) for an iBPGM-type method under a relative-type stopping criterion that allows \(\Delta_k\ne 0\), i.e., remove the restriction \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) while keeping the criterion practically verifiable and ensuring convergence guarantees.
location_in_paper: Page 9, Section 3 (discussion following Algorithm 1; paragraph comparing (AbSC) vs (ReSC))
open_problem_quote: “In our current convergence analysis, we also require \(\|\Delta_k\| = 0\) for (ReSC) to eliminate the error term \(|\langle\Delta_k, \tilde x_{k+1} - x\rangle|\) in (3.7), enabling the derivation of (3.14) in the subsequent analysis for Theorem 3.2. Although this requirement can be satisfied in many cases (see two examples in the following subsection), it may limit the applicability of (ReSC). Note that Bello-Cruz et al. [18] recently incorporated a full relative-type error rule in the FISTA, which can accommodate a nonzero error \(\Delta_k\). Thus, it would be interesting to explore whether the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\| = 0\) can be removed in our methods, which we will leave for future research.”
